claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 3 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 3 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child you're claiming for:**

How old is the child (or children) you want to claim Child Benefit for?

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

Please let me know the age(s) of the child(ren) you're enquiring about, and if possible, their name(s) so I can keep track if there's more than one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I'm looking to claim Child Benefit for my two children - Alex, who is 17, and Blake, who is 3 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'll help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex (17 years old) and Blake (3 years old).

Since your children are in different age categories, let me work through each one:

---

**For Blake (3 years old):**

Since Blake is under 16, they automatically meet the age requirement. Let me check the next criteria for Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: For Blake at 3 years old, I can safely assume they're not working 24+ hours per week. Let me continue checking Blake's eligibility.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: At 3 years old, Blake wouldn't be receiving these benefits. Continuing...

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Blake at 3 years old is not in an apprenticeship. Now let me check the claimant (you) requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good progress! Now I need to ask you some questions that apply to **you as the claimant** (these will apply to your claim for both children).

---

**Residency Question:**

Do you meet **any one** of the following residency requirements?

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. You have **settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have **pre-settled status** and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have **pre-settled status** and are actively seeking work
5. You have **pre-settled status** with sufficient financial resources
6. You have **pre-settled status** and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You have **pre-settled status** as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. You are a **Crown servant** living abroad

Do any of these apply to you? (The most common answer is simply that you normally live in the UK)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I normally live in the UK.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thank you! Since you normally live in the UK, you meet the residency requirements. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Responsibility Question:**

Are you responsible for Alex and Blake? This means **any one** of the following:

1. **You live with the child/children**
2. **You contribute to their upkeep** (paying at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards their care - including food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)
3. **You pay maintenance** under a court order or agreement

Do any of these apply to you for both Alex and Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, both Alex and Blake live with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's straightforward - since both children live with you, you meet the responsibility requirement for both of them. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Other Claimants Question:**

Is anyone else currently claiming (or planning to claim) Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?

- **No** - no one else is claiming for either child
- **Yes, but we've agreed** - someone else was claiming but you've agreed that you should be the one to claim
- **Yes, and there's a dispute** - someone else is claiming and you haven't agreed on who should receive it

Which applies to your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, no one else is claiming for either child.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Good to know. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Child's Location Question:**

Are Alex and Blake currently living in any of these special circumstances?

- **In hospital** - the child is staying in hospital
- **In care** - the child is in local authority care
- **Fostered** - the child is in a foster care arrangement
- **Normal living** - the child is living at home with you (or in normal circumstances)

Which applies to Alex and Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: They're both living normally at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Local Council Support Question:**

Is the local council **NOT** paying towards either child's accommodation or maintenance?

(In other words, you're not receiving local council payments specifically for housing or maintaining Alex or Blake?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, the council isn't paying anything towards their accommodation or maintenance.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! That completes the eligibility checks for Blake (3 years old).

Now I need to go back and check the additional requirements for **Alex (17 years old)**, since children aged 16-19 have extra criteria around education or training.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question about Alex (17 years old):**

Since Alex is 17, I need to check their education or training status. Which of the following applies to Alex?

1. **In education or training** - Alex is currently in approved education or training (e.g., A-levels, T-levels, NVQs, school sixth form, college)
2. **Recently left education** - Alex has left education or training within the last 20 weeks
3. **Neither** - Alex is not in education/training and hasn't been recently

Which applies to Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Neither - Alex isn't in education or training and hasn't been recently.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough to give you a complete eligibility assessment for both children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment Results

### ✅ Blake (3 years old) — **ELIGIBLE**

Great news! You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake. Blake meets all the requirements:
- Under 16 years old (automatic age qualification)
- Lives with you
- No other claimant
- No local council payments involved

### ❌ Alex (17 years old) — **NOT ELIGIBLE**

Unfortunately, you are **not eligible** to claim Child Benefit for Alex at this time.

**Reason:** For children aged 16-19, Child Benefit can only be claimed if they are:
- In **approved education or training** (e.g., A-levels, T-levels, NVQs, school sixth form, college), OR
- Have **recently left education** (within 20 weeks) and registered with a careers service or armed services

Since Alex is not in education or training and hasn't been recently, they don't qualify as a "qualifying child" for Child Benefit purposes.

---

### Next Steps

**For Blake:**
You can apply for Child Benefit online at [www.gov.uk/child-benefit](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit)

**For Alex:**
If Alex's situation changes and they return to approved education or training (before age 20), you may become eligible to claim for them again at that point.

---

Is there anything else you'd like me to clarify about your Child Benefit eligibility?

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old, which falls into the 16-19 age category. For children in this age group, Child Benefit can only be claimed if they are in approved education or training (such as A-levels, T-levels, or NVQs) or have recently left education (within 20 weeks) and registered with a careers service. Since Alex is not currently in education or training and hasn't been recently, Alex does not meet the 'qualifying child' criteria for Child Benefit.

Blake is 3 years old, which is under 16 and automatically meets the age requirement. Blake is not working 24+ hours per week, is not receiving benefits in their own right, is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship, lives with the claimant (who normally resides in the UK), no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake, Blake is living normally at home, and the local council is not paying for Blake's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are satisfied.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant normally lives in the UK and is responsible for both children (who live with them). No other person is claiming Child Benefit for either child, and the local council is not contributing to their care. Blake (3 years old) meets all eligibility requirements as a child under 16. However, Alex (17 years old) does not qualify because they are not in approved education or training and have not recently left education, which is required for children aged 16-19.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.reason: No qualifying child under 16, or under 20 in approved education/training
response.guidance: Child must be under 16, or aged 16-19 and in approved education or training. If child just turned 20, benefit stops. If 16-17 year old leaves education, benefit can continue for 20 weeks if they register with careers service or armed services.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 17 years old, which falls into the 16-19 age category. For children in this age group, Child Benefit can only be claimed if they are in approved education or training (such as A-levels, T-levels, or NVQs) or have recently left education (within 20 weeks) and registered with a careers service. Since Alex is not currently in education or training and hasn't been recently, Alex does not meet the 'qualifying child' criteria for Child Benefit.

Blake is 3 years old, which is under 16 and automatically meets the age requirement. Blake is not working 24+ hours per week, is not receiving benefits in their own right, is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship, lives with the claimant (who normally resides in the UK), no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake, Blake is living normally at home, and the local council is not paying for Blake's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are satisfied.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant normally lives in the UK and is responsible for both children (who live with them). No other person is claiming Child Benefit for either child, and the local council is not contributing to their care. Blake (3 years old) meets all eligibility requirements as a child under 16. However, Alex (17 years old) does not qualify because they are not in approved education or training and have not recently left education, which is required for children aged 16-19.
